coredata.dc:title: Relative humidity sensor based on small up-tapered photonic crystal fiber Mach–Zehnder interferometer
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Rui-jie
Zhao, Yong
Chen, Mao-qing
Peng, Yun
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a new in-line mode Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) based on photonic crystal fiber (PCF) coated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film for relative humidity (RH) sensing was designed. The MZI was formed by splicing a section of PCF between two sections of single mode fiber (SMF) with two up-tapers. The two up-tapers acted as mode splitter/combiner and were made by fusion tapering technique. The Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) incorporated inter modal interference between core mode and cladding modes of the PCF. The refractive index (RI) and volume of GQDs-PVA film will decrease and expansion with the increase of RH, respectively. By measuring the wavelength shift of the interference pattern, RH measurement could be achieved. Before RH measurement, the external RI was tested experimentally to verify that the interference spectrum shifted to right with the increase of refractive index (RI), and the sensitivity was up to 343.7 nm/RIU. In addition, the RH sensing properties with the different thickness of GQDs-PVA film (2.48 μm, 3.72 μm, 4.45 μm, 5.96 μm, 7.42 μm, 8.17 μm) were also investigated experimentally, and the corresponding sensitivity were -0.0901 nm/%RH, −0.0797 nm/%RH, -0.0337 nm/%RH, 0.0586 nm/%RH, 0.0539 nm/%RH, 0.0313 nm/%RH, respectively. The measurement result revealed that different film thickness had different sensitivity and spectrum movement direction. Meanwhile, the sensor had the advantages of simple structure, small size, high sensitivity and low cost. What’s more, the sensor also presents good stability and reversibility of the sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photonic crystal fiber
Mach–Zehnder interferometer
Humidity sensor
Small sensor
GQDs-PVA film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication of the sensing structure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 61425003 and 61773102 , the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant N160408001 , N170407005 and part by the State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries under Grant 2013ZCX09 .

Yong Zhao received his M.A. and Ph.D. degrees, respectively, in precision instrument & automatic measurement with laser and fiber-optic techniques from the Harbin Institute of Technology, China, in 1998 and 2001. He was awarded a first prize scholarship in 2000 by the China Instrument and Control Society and the Sintered Metal Corporation (SMC) scholarship in Japan. He was a scholarship in Japan. He was a postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Electronic Engineering of Tsinghua University from 2001 to 2003, and then worked as an associate professor in the Department of Automation, Tsinghua University of China. In 2006, he was a visiting scholar of University of Illinois in Urbana and Champagne, USA. In 2008, he was awarded as the “New Century Excellent Talents in University” by the Ministry of Education of China. In 2009, he was awarded as the “Liaoning Bai-Qian-Wan Talents” by Liaoning Province. In 2011, he was awarded by the Royal Academy of Engineering as an academic research fellow of City University London. In 2014, he was awarded by the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China. In 2015, he was honored as the Yangtze River Scholar Distinguished Professor by the Ministry of Education of China. Now he is working in Northeastern University as a full professor. As the academic leader and director of his research institute, his current research interests are the development of fiber-optic sensors and device, fiber Bragg grating sensors, novel sensor materials and principles, slow light and sensor technology, optical measurement technologies. He has authored and co-authored more than 260 scientific papers and conference presentations, 23 patents, and 5 books. He is a member in the Editorial Boards of the international journals of Sensor Letters, Instrumentation Science & Technology, Journal of Sensor Technology, and Advances in Optical Technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the designed sensor probe.

The sensing probe making process: (a) the cross section diagram of PCF; (b) microscope diagram of a lateral view PCF; (c) microscope diagram of a lateral view SMF; (d) sensor probe fabrication by fusion splitter; (e) microscope diagram of one up-taper between PCF and SMF.
Output spectrum of proposed sensor for different lengths of PCF.
Relationship between coating film thickness and dip-coating velocity.
Diagram of experimental system connection; (a) amplified spontaneous emission (ASE); (b) optical spectrum analyzer (OSA); (c) Humidity generating unit; (c) Hygrometer.
Transmission spectra of proposed sensor with different refractive index. Insert is the linear fitting between RH and wavelength shift of corresponding finger.

Changes of interference spectra coated with different thickness of GQDs-PVA film: (a) 2.48μm, (b) 3.72μm, (c) 4.45μm, (d) 4.45 μm, (e) 5.96 μm, 7.42 μm, (f) 8.17 μm. Insert is the linear fitting between RH and wavelength shift of corresponding finger.
(b) Reversibility of the sensor. The thickness of the GQDs-PVA film is 3.72 μm there.
Transmission spectra of proposed sensor with 3.72 μm thickness of GQDs-PVA film at different temperature. Insert is the linear fitting between RH and wavelength shift of corresponding finger.
The corresponding capability of proposed RH sensor with different GQDs-PVA film thickness.

head.ce:title: Relative humidity sensor based on small up-tapered photonic crystal fiber Mach–Zehnder interferometer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a new in-line mode Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) based on photonic crystal fiber (PCF) coated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film for relative humidity (RH) sensing was designed. The MZI was formed by splicing a section of PCF between two sections of single mode fiber (SMF) with two up-tapers. The two up-tapers acted as mode splitter/combiner and were made by fusion tapering technique. The Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) incorporated inter modal interference between core mode and cladding modes of the PCF. The refractive index (RI) and volume of GQDs-PVA film will decrease and expansion with the increase of RH, respectively. By measuring the wavelength shift of the interference pattern, RH measurement could be achieved. Before RH measurement, the external RI was tested experimentally to verify that the interference spectrum shifted to right with the increase of refractive index (RI), and the sensitivity was up to 343.7 nm/RIU. In addition, the RH sensing properties with the different thickness of GQDs-PVA film (2.48 μm, 3.72 μm, 4.45 μm, 5.96 μm, 7.42 μm, 8.17 μm) were also investigated experimentally, and the corresponding sensitivity were -0.0901 nm/%RH, −0.0797 nm/%RH, -0.0337 nm/%RH, 0.0586 nm/%RH, 0.0539 nm/%RH, 0.0313 nm/%RH, respectively. The measurement result revealed that different film thickness had different sensitivity and spectrum movement direction. Meanwhile, the sensor had the advantages of simple structure, small size, high sensitivity and low cost. What’s more, the sensor also presents good stability and reversibility of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: The sensors based on optical fibers have many advantages compared with traditional electric sensors such as anti-electromagnetic interference, compact structure, high accuracy and good stability, and have been applied to different physical quantities and chemical measurements such as temperature, gases, PHs and humidity [1–6]. The real-time monitoring of relative humidity (RH) is essential in food processing, bio-pharming, medicine and agriculture production [7,8]. Nowadays, many optical fiber sensors have been proposed for the measurement of RH, which includes fiber Bragg gratings (FBG), interference, lossy mode resonances (LMRs), micro-knot resonators and whispering galleries modes (WGM) [9–13]. Among them, FBG and interference are the main research types. FBGs have attract great attentions due to their inherent multiplexing capability and reusable. While interferometer is easy to get and simple operation. In addition, hygroscopic materials will expands with the increasing of RH values, which will apply pressure to FBG and realize the measurement of RH [14–16]. In [17] coated with polyimide by dip-coating method and the sensitivity range from 1.4 to 5.6 pm/%RH depending on the thickness of the coating. NTs, coated onto an etched FBG, provided the highest sensitivity found for conventional optical fiber humidity sensor (OFHS), which was 31 pm/%RH [18]. However, etching of the cladding of a FBG can enhance the sensitivity, while the etching process made them fragile and influence their long-term stability. What’s more, the demodulation device of FBG was expensive that made it unpractical in real practical applications. Optical fibers could generate many degrees of modal interferometers by changing interference structures, and many structures for RH sensors have been proposed. Juliana S. Santos [19] reported an optical fiber Fabry–Perot interferometry for humidity sensing with the RH range from 22 to 80% by analysing the correspondent reflection spectra of the interference fringes, whose sensitivity was 3.5 nm/%RH. However, the FP interferometer was hard to recover and the demodulation method was not intuitionistic. Li-Peng Sun [20] reported a high-birefringence microfiber Sagnac interferometer based humidity sensor and the sensitivity can reach to 422.2 pm/%RH with the measurement range from 30%RH to 90%RH. While the length of Panda fiber was about 14 cm, which was not appropriate for local or single point measurement. A low temperature-sensitivity relative humidity sensor based on taper square no-core fiber coated with SiO2 nanoparticles was reported [21], and the sensitivity could reached to 584.2 pm/%RH with the measurement range from 43.6 to 98.6%RH, but its testing result was not linearity. M. R. K. Soltanian [22] proposed a variable waist-diameter Mach–Zehnder tapered interferometer as humidity and temperature sensor with the sensitivity of 0.02 nm/%RH in the range of 30 to 70%RH, while the length of the sensing probe was 140 mm. Therefore, high sensitivity and small size RH sensors are needed at present.

In this paper, an interferometric RH sensor based on PCF with GQDs-PVA compound is proposed and experimentally demonstrated. Up-taper between SMF-PCF is used as the sensing carrier to inspire the cladding model. The RH-sensitive film is coated on the surface of PCF. The refractive of GQDs-PVA sensitive film will be decrease and the volume will expanse with the increase of the RH values [22,23], which will affect the structural parameters of PCF and then cause the change of the output spectrum. Based on this explanation, the relationship between RH values and the spectra shift can be established. Besides, the effects of different thickness of GQDs-PVA film on the RH sensitivity is studied to identify the optimum thickness of GQDs-PVA for RH measurement. What’s more, the stability and reversibility are also tested, and temperature effect is also considered.

The structure of such a sensor probe is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . The input light output from broadband light broadband amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) source enters into SMF will be divided into two beams when the input light enters into the first up-taper region, one of the two beams couples into the cladding and the other beam coupling into the fiber core as the fundamental mode. In the second up-tapered area, the energy of some cladding modes coupled into the core, and then the interference generate.

As the effective RI is different between the core mode and cladding modes which is caused by the changing of RH, phase delay can appear after the same propagation distance, and then stabilization of interference spectrum is formed that can be observed through optical spectral analyzer (OSA). In sensing region, the cladding modes are always there, which fits with the principle of Mach–Zehnder double-beam interference. The interference intensity between fundamental mode and cladding mode can be expressed as [24]:
(1)
I =
I 1
+
I 2
+ 2
I 1
I 2
c o s ( Δ φ + δ )
where
I 1
and
I 2
are the transport mode in light intensity and cladding mode of transmission of light intensity; φ is phase of light; and δ is the initial phase difference. The phase delay and center wavelength can be represented as:
(2)
Δ φ =
2 π (
n
e f f
c o r e
−
n
e f f
c l a d
) L
λ
=
2 π ( Δ
n
e f f
L )
λ
(3)
λ m
=
Δ
n
e f f
L
m
where
n
e f f
c o r e
and
n
e f f
c l a d
are the effective refractive indexes of fiber core mode and cladding mode, respectively.
Δ
n
e f f
is the difference between
n
e f f
c o r e
and
n
e f f
c l a d
, and λ is the free light wavelength.
λ m
represents the m class interference, while L is the length of interference region. In the interference of the up-tapered structure, where the resonant wavelength
λ m
depends on the thickness and refractive index of the GQDs-PVA film as well. The increase in RH will lead to more nanopores opening in the GQDs-PVA film and more water molecules inserting in GQDs-PVA film because of the super permeability of the GQDs-PVA film. In addition, the increasing RH can also lead to a significant swelling of the GQDs-PVA film, which results in an increase in thickness of the GQDs-PVA film and thus a redshift of the resonant wavelength. At the same time, the refractive index of the GQDs-PVA film will decrease with the increasing of RH, which leads to the left shift of the resonance wavelength. Based on this explanation, the relationship between RH values and the spectra shift could be established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of the sensing structure
ce:section.ce:para: For fabricating the up-tapered interferometer, the ordinary SMF that is offered by Corning Company and a commercial single-mode PCF is adopted with the core diameter 9 μm and the cladding diameter is the same as SMF. In addition, five rings of holes are exist and all air holes diameters are 5 μm. The PCF is provided by Yangtze Optical Electronic Co., Ltd (China, Wuhan), and the cross section diagram is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The making process is shown in Fig. 2(b)–(e). Firstly, a section of SMF and PCF was cut flat by fiber cutting tool, and was shown in Fig. 2(b) and (c), respectively. And then put them to the fusion splitter that is shown in Fig. 2(d), one of the up-taper between SMF and PCF can get by regulating discharge strength and propelling distance of the fusion splitter, which is shown in Fig. 2(e). The other up-taper is also obtained by this way, and then the sensing probe fabrication is finished.

While many parameters will impact the interference spectral lines directly, and the length of PCF in the up-tapered interference structure is required to be optimized as shown in Fig. 3 . The illustration in Fig. 3 shows the physical map of the sensing structure, and the diameter of the up-taper is chosen as 164.10 μm.

In Fig. 3, it can be seen that the spectrum is too wide when the length of PCF is 0.55 cm, which linear fitting is not easy to find the lowest point of the interference spectrum. While the interference spectrum is inhomogeneity and part of the interference valley is not obvious when the length of PCF is 2.25 cm that is not appropriate for optical fiber sensors. Therefore, a length of 1.8 cm is finally selected as the suitable length of PCF for the proposed interferometer.

The PVA material adopted in the experiment is white powder crystal and the degree of polymerization is 1750 ± 750. GQDs solution is bought from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. Concentration and diameter of the adopted GQDs dispersion are 1 mg/ml and 5–15 nm, respectively. There are Carbonyl group, carboxyl group and hydroxyl group on the surface of GQDs, and the Oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs makes it easy to intercalate polar organic molecules as well as polymers between the layers of GQDs. What’s more, Carboxylate groups negatively charged on the surface of GQDs surface is easier to absorbed water molecules than the OH functionalities [25], which means PVA and GQDs can be intensive mixed. At the same time, the volume of the GQDs will be broadened by the intercalated PVA molecules and the hydroxyl (OH) functional groups which introduced by water molecules. According to Ref. [9], the quantities of GQDs and PVA are 9 ml and 161 mg, respectively. The GQDs-PVA compounds is made by magnetic stirrer at room temperature, and then is three hours of quiet to remove the air bubbles in the compounds. The mixture results is shown in Fig. 4 , which indicates the GQDs and PVA are fully mixture.

Before coating film, the fiber cladding was removed and cleaned by alcohol, and then immersed into the GQDs-PVA dispersion at room temperature. The drawing speed of the dip coating machine can be changed before the dip coating process to get different thickness of the coated film. In order to form the stable composite film on the surface of the MZI, the fiber sensor was natural dried at room temperature for 10 h. Each of the coating process is repeated three times, the relationship between the film thickness and the drawing speed is shown in Fig. 5 , and it can be seen that the thickness of GQDs-PVA film is inversely proportional to the speed of the drawing.

Fig. 6 shows the working process of the detection system: wide spectrum light (from amplified spontaneous emission (ASE)) went into the RH chamber and connected with the RH sensitive probes, then the output was tested by optical spectrum analyzer (OSA). The different RH values was generated by different saturated salt solutions, and the detail RH values is calibrated by a hygrometer. What’s more, the experiment is executed at room temperature.

In order to better understand the direction of the spectrum movement, which is caused by the change of the refractive index or the expansion of the spectrum. The RI-sensitive characteristic of the proposed interference structure was studied and tested to verify the variation of spectrum with the refractive index increase before RH sensing material coating. The external RI of the tested liquid could be adjusted from 1.333 to 1.3726 in the experiment.

With the increasing of the external RI, transmitted MZI spectrums were shift to length wavelength, and the results were illustrated in Fig. 7 . Then the fitting curve between the characteristic wavelengths and external RI was plotted in the illustration of Fig. 7, which illustrated the external RI sensitivity was up to 343.7 nm/RIU. In addition, the results indicated that the spectrum shifted to right with the increasing of RI.

After that, the sensor probes coated with GQDs-PVA film of different thickness were tested and recorded, the results were shown in Fig. 8 (a)–(f). Fig. 8(a)–(c) presented the experiment results where the film coating thickness is 2.48 μm, 3.72 μm and 4.45 μm, respectively, which indicates that all the interference spectrum shift to blue light with the increasing of RH. Fig. 8(d)–(f) presented the experiment results where the film coating thickness is 5.96μm, 7.42μm, 8.17μm, respectively, which indicates that all the interference spectrum shift to red light with the increasing of RH. In addition, the illustration in Fig. 8(a)–(f) is the corresponding linear fitting of different interference spectrum, which reveals that the sensitivity of interference dips about at 1600 nm is the highest where the film thickness is 2.48 μm. Observing the interference spectrum of refractive index experiment, we can conclude that: before and after coating, the sensor has four interference dips, and the refractive index experiment indicates that each movement of four interference dips can be used for sensitivity calibration in practical application. Considering the RH sensitivity, the linear fitting between wavelength shift and RH of interference dips about at 1600 nm is adopted for comparison, and the details sensing performance of different thickness coating film are shown in Table 1 .

Comparing the sensitivity with different thickness of GQDs-PVA film, we can conclude that: the interference spectrum blue shift with RH increasing and the sensitivity decreases when the thickness of coating film is less than 4.45μm, the changing direction of interference spectrum is consistent with theoretical analysis of interference index of GQDs-PVA film decrease with the increase of RH. While the interference spectrum red shift when the thickness of coating film is more than 5.96μm, which indicates that the shift of interference spectrum is mainly caused by pressure introduced by the swelling of GQDs-PVA with the increasing of RH. In addition, the highest sensitivity was −0.0901 nm/%RH when the GQDs-PVA film thickness was 2.48μm, which was most suitable for RH measurement.

In addition, the sensitivity is not the only evaluation criterion for the performance of the sensor, stability and reversibility are also important criteria for evaluation. Therefore, 3.72 μm thickness of the GQDs-PVA film is adopted for stability and reversibility experiment. The stability experiment was implemented at room temperature, at 28.73, 47.32 and 75.86%RH, we picked the data every ten minutes in 40 min, and the result was shown in Fig. 9 (a), and the maximum error of 28.73, 47.32 and 75.86 RH values are 0.16 nm, 0.16 nm and 0.136 nm, respectively, which equivalents to the wavelength shift caused by 2%RH, 2%RH and 0.17%RH. Fig. 9(b) is the reversibility experiment results tested at room temperature, and there is almost no slight except at 69.3%RH, where the float is about 0.365 nm, which may be caused by temperature fluctuation or the reading error.

Moreover, the temperature effect is also tested in the experiment, and the result is shown in Fig. 10 , which shows that the interference spectrum blue shift with the temperature increasing from 20℃ to 32℃. The sensitivity of the 0.2356 nm/℃, which reveals that changes in temperature will affect the measurement of RH. To overcome the influence of temperature on the measurement of RH, FBG is not sensitive to other physical or chemical quantities except for temperature, and can be cascaded with the proposed sensor for temperature sensing to eliminate the effect of temperature [26]. In addition, comparing with other interference sensing structures, interference peak of FBG is sharp which makes it easy to find peak in sensitivity demodulation. What’s more, FBG is already a very mature commercial product, we only need to connect the proposed RH sensor and FBG together and can overcome the problem of temperature crosstalk. Except for FBG, we also can weld the proposed RH sensor with other interference structures, such as a section of hollow core fiber that is filled with alcohol to compensate temperature crosstalk, where the hollow core fiber filled with alcohol will shift to blue wavelength with temperature increasing, and the final demodulation of RH sensitivity and temperature sensitivity is based on a simultaneous sensitivity, and the feasibility of the second method also has been verified in our previous work [27]. However, compared with first method, the second method needs an alcohol filling process, which will increase the difficulty of making the sensor probe. In addition, the making process of filling alcohol in a section of hollow core fiber hasn’t yet reached the level of commercialization, which means the temperature compensate structure may not be exactly the same at each making. Therefore, the first method is the optimal choice to compensate temperature crosstalk.

In summary, a miniature sensor with a structure of splicing regions tapered PCF based on MZI was proposed and demonstrated in this paper. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film acted as humidity sensitive material, the RI decreases and volume decreases and expand with RH increasing, and was coated on the surface of PCF. The sensor structure parameter was discussed and the optimal sensing structure parameters were following: length of PCF was 1.8 cm and Taper waist diameter was 164.10 μm. Before RH experimental, the external RI experiment was executed, and the sensitivity was up to 343.7 nm/RIU, which indicated that the spectrum shifted to right with the increase of RI. The RH sensitivity of proposed sensor coated with different thickness of GQDs-PVA film (2.48 μm, 3.72 μm, 4.45 μm, 5.96 μm, 7.42 μm, 8.17 μm)were −0.0901 nm/%RH, −0.0797 nm/%RH, −0.0337 nm/%RH, 0.0586 nm/%RH, 0.0539 nm/%RH, 0.0313 nm/%RH, respectively, which revealed that the spectrum shift to blue light when the film thickness is less than 4.45 μm, and the sensitivity could be up to −0.0901 nm/%RH with the measurement range changing from 13.77%RH to 77.87%RH, which meant that the RI change of GQDs-PVA played a leading role in the measurement process. While the spectrum shift to red light when the film thickness is more than 4.45 μm, and the sensitivity could be up to 0.0586 nm/%RH, which meant the volume change of GQDs-PVA played a main role in the measurement process. Meanwhile, the experiment results revealed that the film thickness would impact spectrum movement direction and the sensitivity. Meanwhile, it also indicated that the most suitable film thickness for sensing was 2.48 μm. In addition, the small size of the sensor probe made it practical. Except for the small size, simple making process and costless of the sensor, the highlight of this manuscript is the discussion of effects of film coating thickness on RH sensing performance, and the good stability and reversibility of the sensor. At the same time, the method to overcome temperature crosstalk is provided.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 61425003 and 61773102, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant N160408001, N170407005 and part by the State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries under Grant 2013ZCX09.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rui-jie Tong was born in Henan, China, in December 1991. She is currently working toward the PhD degree at College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, China. Currently her research is focused on the design and fabrication of the photonic crystal fiber sensors and their applications.

Yong Zhao received his M.A. and Ph.D. degrees, respectively, in precision instrument & automatic measurement with laser and fiber-optic techniques from the Harbin Institute of Technology, China, in 1998 and 2001. He was awarded a first prize scholarship in 2000 by the China Instrument and Control Society and the Sintered Metal Corporation (SMC) scholarship in Japan. He was a scholarship in Japan. He was a postdoctoral fellow in the Department of Electronic Engineering of Tsinghua University from 2001 to 2003, and then worked as an associate professor in the Department of Automation, Tsinghua University of China. In 2006, he was a visiting scholar of University of Illinois in Urbana and Champagne, USA. In 2008, he was awarded as the “New Century Excellent Talents in University” by the Ministry of Education of China. In 2009, he was awarded as the “Liaoning Bai-Qian-Wan Talents” by Liaoning Province. In 2011, he was awarded by the Royal Academy of Engineering as an academic research fellow of City University London. In 2014, he was awarded by the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China. In 2015, he was honored as the Yangtze River Scholar Distinguished Professor by the Ministry of Education of China. Now he is working in Northeastern University as a full professor. As the academic leader and director of his research institute, his current research interests are the development of fiber-optic sensors and device, fiber Bragg grating sensors, novel sensor materials and principles, slow light and sensor technology, optical measurement technologies. He has authored and co-authored more than 260 scientific papers and conference presentations, 23 patents, and 5 books. He is a member in the Editorial Boards of the international journals of Sensor Letters, Instrumentation Science & Technology, Journal of Sensor Technology, and Advances in Optical Technologies.

Mao-qing Chen was born in Liaoning, China, in Oct. 1992. He received his B.A. degrees in the College of Information Science and Engineering from the Shenyang University of Technology, China, in 2015. He is now a PhD student of Northeastern University. His research interests are optical fiber sensors, optical modal interference sensors, optoelectronic measurement technology and system.

Yun Peng was born in Hebei, China, in Jan. 1992. He received his B.A. degrees in the School of Mechanical Engineering from the Hebei University of Science and Technology, China, in 2015. He received his M.A. degrees in the School of Mechanical Engineering and Automation from the Northeastern University, China, in 2017. He is now a PhD student of Northeastern University. His research interests are optical microfiber sensors, optoelectronic measurement technology and system.
